sentences.text: Yes.

That was a sort of an accidental-- and I think I have to determine just how-- how-- how it happened, since I was-- I was an organizer, a full-time organizer in western-- in western Canada for an organization, cultural organization, that, see, benefit organization, and in the meantime being a member of the Communist Party, you see, where you are always organized.
Well, it-- it wasn't so-- so open.
But we were also organizing that-- a function of in this leftist organization where almost all the members of the-- of the Communist Party, you see.
That's why we were so-- so effective.
We are leading the great, great struggle in the hungry thirties, you see.
It went down in history as the hungry thirties.
It was-- it was our job.
And then I came down for the convention.
I was called down from my central office to help to organize that-- that convention in October.
And arrived-- arrived here on the-- on the 30th of-- 30th of August and was on a Saturday.
The exhibition was still on.
They showed me-- took me out to the exhibition.
That was the last day.
There was an exhibition on a Sunday at-- at that time.

So the-- the-- the day passed, and I got acquainted with a few of the leading-- the leading people in Hungarian-- Hungarian organization because they had a club here and they took me in the-- in the evening.
And then Sunday passed and so on.
And Monday morning, I was going down to the editorial office of our paper, The Canadian-Hungarian [INAUDIBLE].
And then all of a sudden in a street corner and an intersection there's somebody's calling my name.
I didn't-- who could be calling me in-- in-- Toronto here?
So turned around, and there is this fellow I already knew.
He was introduced on Sunday.
He was driving a-- a-- a-- a bread truck as a-- a delivery-- he said, Andy, Andy, you know what's the news?
Very excitingly and so.
What?
What?
What's the news?
He said, the war broke out.
He said, Germany invaded Poland.
And it was-- it was September the 1st already.
And-- but he had a radio, and he was-- he was up at 3 o'clock to deliver bread to the house, you know?
So he-- he had-- he had [? affair ?] news.
It-- it was really a shock.
And then on the double, hurry, on the double I ran down to the-- to Wellington Street from Dundas and-- Dundas.
And-- and that's my area and told people there.
Some there went in the early [INAUDIBLE].
And they didn't hear it when I told them.
Well, they smiled.
They thought I am making a joke.
But didn't take really very long, only 10, 15 minutes.
They all knew.
And then it was a shock.
Well, what to do now?
We knew.
We knew.
There's a war.
There's a war time on.
That-- the leftist movement will be under-- under pressure.
And so-- and sure enough, sure enough, by October came-- came a order in council in Ottawa, part of the-- the government.
First the Communist Party and all these leftist organizations were outlawed.
But-- but two [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] [INAUDIBLE] organization was spared, because it was a government-chartered organization.
But the cultural organization was banned.
But all paper, all paper were spared because the RCMP went around, you see, to all the-- they had the [INAUDIBLE].
And they-- they didn't come to us, to our paper and the Wochenblatt.
You ever heard of the Wochenblatt?
It's like the paper of the week.
Is that it?
Yeah, it was a Jewish leftist paper under Joe-- Joe Gershman and Sam Lipschitz.
I don't know if you ever heard of this because they were leading-- leading party members and elitists of the-- in the-- in the Jewish progressive, leftist movement.
And then I found out only-- I have to tell you this.
Two years later, how did it happen?
And every week when we closed the papers, it was-- at that time, we were already just a weekly paper.
Every-- after event on the press, I was in charge, because the leading-- leading people, leading personnel on our paper and our organization, they just-- they just disappeared.
They went hiding before they were arrested and then interned.
You heard about this internment in-- in Petawawa.
We were only [? six ?] [? of the ?] leading people in the Communist Party and this leading leftist organization were interned.
And so I was-- I was left there.
I was-- I was holding the management of the paper.
And when I finished the paper, I just-- I just disappeared, because it went right to the-- right to the-- right to the censor, the paper.
And-- and-- and they censored the papers.
And was so something, you see-- put a-- a padlock on it.
And we just kept surviving, and we went along and along, and soon found out that-- that outside was [INAUDIBLE].
Call it the Wochenblatt, is that thing.
I looked up the [INAUDIBLE] there.
How-- how-- how come?
Well, they couldn't give it an answer.
And-- and might as well tell you now how it happened.
In 1939, in the summer of 1939, somebody knocked on-- on the door of-- no, 1940.
Knocked at the-- on the door of the editorial office.
And there-- there walked in a young man about your-- your size and your-- your complexion and everything else.
He was about-- he was about 24 or 25.
And he introduces himself.
He says, from Slovakia.
He's from Slovakia.
And without really any question, well, he's-- I'm an-- I'm a refugee and escapee.
I escaped from the-- from the-- from the German occupation.
Said, I'm Joe.
And so, my god.
And well, we embraced the guy.
What-- what a rare bird coming in and so on.
And so we make friends soon.
And he wrote very, very good articles and making social and economic-- economic-- economic problems.
And then at one-- at one day, he just simply disappeared completely.
Never knew what happened to him.
Only two years later-- no, about three years later, we had the first Hungarian embassy opened up.
And they-- they-- they led me to the-- they-- they-- they knew this man.
And then finally, I met up with him.
And then he told me that they-- that they knew when he came to Canada.
And he was under-- yeah, he was on the list, you see.
And he was an educated man, a very educated man, speaking four or five languages.
And took him up to-- up to-- to Ottawa, into censor's office.
And what he did, he did the dirty work.
He just overlooked.
He also-- also knew Yiddish.
The paper, the Wochenblatt, was a Yiddish paper.
He knew Hungarian.
He just overlooked.
He just-- he just-- he just fooled the RCMP, the censor's office.
That's how he survived.
So these kind of things could come to the-- they come to the open now.
Yeah.
So that-- that-- that's how things-- that things happened.
So you survived, the-- the paper.
Well, first I was drafted into the British Secret Service in-- in about June, right-- right in sometimes in the middle of June.
I never kept these-- these dates exactly.
But it was in the-- sometime in the middle of June in 1942.
I had a-- I had a telephone call from the-- from the party office.
When I-- when I'm talking party, it's always the Communist Party.
If this-- something else I hear, I'll identify.
From the department of the-- of the party of the-- of the ethnic section of the-- of the Communist Party had a telephone call that I should go to a certain place on Bay Street at so and such hour.
And just-- just below Queen Street on-- on-- on Bay-- Bay Street there.

And then, when I-- where I went right at the door, walking-- walking in, another Hungarian who was-- who was the second secretary of our-- of our cultural organization who worked in the national office.
And really old, maybe two years old, older than-- than I.
So we just walked up together in a given room.
There was a bare table there, bare naked table, not woman.
And-- and-- and two chairs there, and one behind-- one behind the table there.
And we just waited a few minutes and-- and the door opens and somebody walks in, sits behind the table, ask our names.
You are so?
Yes.
And that's so?
Yes.
And then he started right off.
I am here from-- from England.
I'm here from-- from-- from Mr.
Churchill, the prime minister himself, personally.
And I have a certain-- certain mission to fulfill.
I tell you straight.
Mr.
Churchill needs people like you.
We didn't catch on right away.
We'd be like you-- why-- why-- why-- why us?
And then soon it came that he-- I'm here to-- to recruit people from your party, from the-- from the-- from the-- from the Communist Party.
And well, his-- his assignment-- assignment was actually first to see Mackenzie King, the prime minister, and through Mackenzie King find the leader of the Communist Party, Mr.
Tim Buck.

And here again, it's in-- in my book that-- I didn't put it in that way, but his instruction-- instruction was to avoid the RCMP because Churchill knew that this backward [? bigotness ?] of the-- the-- the Victorian mentality of the RCMP.
And he knew that there going to be trouble.
How come they-- they have the Communist Party all [INAUDIBLE] and-- and we want to recruit it.
[LAUGHS] So this can complicate situation.
But then finally-- finally he-- he found Tim Buck through-- through the paper.
It was a different story.
There's a whole-- whole book about it.
I-- it's not my-- my-- my subject here.
But anyway, finally-- finally the-- this agent from the British Secret Service-- the Special Operation Executive was the full name of it-- found Tim Buck.
And this agent had-- had a-- a meeting with Tim Buck.
As a matter of fact, when they found him he was-- he was hiding out just not far from here in-- in Stouffville in a [INAUDIBLE] very [? white ?] farm, with a-- with a farm.
And that-- that's where they-- they met.
And then he was asked to-- to give him members of his party for such and such a purpose, for a special assignment behind the German lines.
So we need people who could be trusted and who would go through physical and mental test for this kind of-- this kind of assignment and this kind of training and-- and operation.
So then finally-- finally, when Tim Buck then supplied first Paul Phillips, who was also-- also Jewish, immigrated.
I think he came in '27, '28 in Canada.
He was-- he was the head of the-- of the ethnic department of the party.
He took a national tour, you see, and he picked the proper people, you see.
And we were very-- you know, the first were the Yugoslav groups-- 22 of them, the Croatian service and the Slovenians and so on.
They were trained and then out at Camp X.
That Camp X, it's between Whitby and-- and-- and Oshawa, that secret training camp.
And second group were Hungarians.
And [? one ?] in nine was landed in-- in one group.
And they never trained two separate groups together.
It was always-- it was always a rule and a policy that the least people you know is the better.
So that they just trained one-- one-- one group at-- at a time.
And when we were approached-- when we were approached, it was 1922-- 1942-- with this agent what I told you about.
And then it was all.
We sensed that-- that our destination is Hungary.
The [INAUDIBLE] the question, he never told us--
No, not-- not that.
But this was all our education, our knowledge of the language.
And a lot like you, you were asking me about my-- my knowledge about-- about the country and my-- my level of education and so on.
How well I know the history of the country.
And after these-- these questions, well, we sensed it very real.
We-- we have some kind of military-- military assignment to go-- to go to Hungary.
And then we shook hand and never-- and never a single word.
And it came way up to the time.
Well, it came-- it came December and nothing again.
And, well, people were young.
People were flocking into the army.
And at one time in our top-- top leadership that I was a member of-- of the organization, simply told to welcome it.
I'm ready to-- to join the army and sometime and soon.
So look around to-- to replace me.
Every day they're going and we have to show the way for the Hungarian young people to go into the army.
And because we have to-- we have to fulfill the things what we are preaching, antifascism.
And then it is the time now into the army and arms.
And then it was in December.
It was in December.
And then I reported to the military district and an exhibition around the-- the Canadian army.
I was called in the first weekend in January.
I was-- I was-- I was out in the basic training camp and was trained in a basic.
And from there--
No, I volunteered.
I was a volunteer, volunteered for the Canadian Army.
And because-- well, all I did at the interview, thought, well, six months has passed-- passed already, and-- but it's a long, long story behind it.
But partly, it's in the introduction of my-- on my book.
Yeah.
Because the British were all so uncertain about Horthy.
Horthy was slimy, you see.
And-- and-- but finally-- finally, then, Horthy made up his-- his mind that he's going to side with the-- with the Allies.
But-- but with the British or Americans?
Churchill told him, no, no, no, no, Mr.
Horthy, you [? bleed ?] on your army, or keep your [? army ?] assigned with the Red Army.
That's where your army's fighting.
That's why you had to turn around.
That's how it-- I see.
It worked.
So Churchill was a realistic-- realistic person.
Yes, yes.
Well-- well, we figured six months has passed and-- and there is no call for the secret kind of service or whatever it is.
So-- so I joined the army.
And we have to show-- show the way from the-- from the top to-- to Hungarian young people that we are joining, we take it seriously.
This is the time, what-- what we have heard and [INAUDIBLE] because we had youth club and so, and we had the [INAUDIBLE] Communist League and the Hungarian Youth.
Well, this is your chance.
Give your arm at the very first chance you get.
Well, I was in Orillia, just north of here, Orillia.
There was a basic training camp there.
It lasted for-- for six weeks.
And from there, I chose where I joined up the Canadian armored corps, the tank corps.
And then I was shipped from basic, and then they dispersed us into the units that we-- that we chose voluntarily.
And I was-- and I was shipped on to Camp Borden.
That was the-- the tank corps training.
And we were trained, not fully, because then the finer things was done in-- done in England.
And came-- came June, I was in the-- in the trained soldier's regiment, they called it.
We were waiting for draft to be shipped over to England.
And while waiting there, all of us, you see, nothing to do and so on, and came an alarm in the-- the whole camp.
A jeep was going around, loud speaker calling my name, Andrew Durovecz, Andrew Durovecz, all around.
And I was-- four of us from our troop.
And we were on-- on camp duty at-- at-- at-- on the day.
Yeah, because [INAUDIBLE] on, and we'd just done it fast.
It was real, real hot on that June day.
And just moved out to the edge of the camp and a lot-- a lot done on the-- the street, [INAUDIBLE] and so on.
And somebody went, hey, they're calling your name.
So-- so what?
So I said, oh.
He's coming closer, he's calling my name.
And then on the double, in the direction of [INAUDIBLE] we ended up and they were looking for you, come and come, come.
They dragged me into the-- into the jeep and right-- right into the regimental office.
There was a colonel.
And he laid it down.
It was-- it was already-- already-- by that time already, I should have been in Toronto.
But the orderly had misplaced the paper-- they called it paper-- an urgent paper for the colonel when he came back.
When he was going through the paper, it was in the bottom.
And [INAUDIBLE] and so on to be-- to be discharged from Camp Borden had already been sent into number two district in [INAUDIBLE] to Toronto.
And it was already 2 o'clock in the afternoon.
I should have been in Toronto.
He came himself and packed my-- my-- my bag so in the--  in the barrack and-- and fast.
The back pay and so on, and-- and threw me in the-- it's all-- all-- all in the book-- in a jeep and way down to Toronto.
And they already ordered the other eight were-- I knew every one of them except one.
They were all members of our organization.
Then I suspected, that must be something that we were on [INAUDIBLE] for.
And sure enough, it-- it was.
And there they assigned me-- assigned me all work.
Took everything from my kit, my army kit, except the-- the things that you've-- you've got to get on yourself.
You're not going to need it.
And they jammed us into-- into a car and then into a jeep, and out, started out to the east.
I'm coming to it.
We-- we didn't know.
And we talked as well, by god, they-- they taking us over to Montreal.
Or maybe-- maybe not to Montreal, maybe just to-- to Kingston.
And as we come past Whitby, a couple-- a couple more miles, a dirt road, a farmer road just [INAUDIBLE].
And the jeep just tears up the dirt on dirt road.
Well, where the hell are we going?
And then all of a sudden we come to a-- came to a-- a huge sign there, huge sign.
This-- this-- this area, or this property-- how did it say-- is under the-- under the protection of national defense.
And as we looked at the sign or whatever, a-- a guard with a bayonet that we had to stop, but he knew that the-- the drivers and so on had opened up the gate and drived into.
But it-- it was really a crummy place.
You see, all the rough buildings and-- and structures and so on.
And then we just registered and tried to really talk.
And the rest of it you-- you-- you read in my book.
How this-- how this tough to train.
And it was tough training there, right on-- like, right on Lake Ontario.
It was a farm, about a 200-acre farm, And the whole area, it was useful for these kind of things.
And-- and there was no-- no ifs and buts.
But all the trainings.
It was live ammunition in the-- in the guns, live hand grenades and everything, explosive, live.
Once we made an-- an explosion there, it broke the windows, the shop windows in the outskirts of-- of Oshawa by accident, blow up.
Not in so many words.
But when the first evening-- the first evening, then they took us out into-- into-- into bedrooms and so on.
And that was a small room.
We waited there, and then they opened-- opened up the-- the reception room, the mess room for-- for-- for supper.
When they opened it, we walked in, oh, my god.
We were right in the really [INAUDIBLE] of Hungary.
Had a huge Hungarian map there, and the-- the-- the table-- and the table is covered like with Hungarian handcrafted cloths there.
And the table's loaded with the best Hungarian wine, and Hungarian wine, and Hungarian breads and so on.
My god, we just stopped dead and almost shocked.
And we really had a hell of an evening with the-- with the officers there.
They didn't have to tell us anything.
We knew.
By that time, everyone knew our destination is Hungary.
That's what we were going to be trained for.
But to how and so on, then-- then next day they started right off.
We were three months.
Well, then-- then we just-- we just packed up.
We had-- we had weekend-- weekend leave.
And well, I had-- I had no relatives or anybody here, just a-- just a passing girlfriend and the members of the-- of the organization and so on.
So we spent Saturday and Sunday, Friday evening, Sunday, Monday-- so Saturday and Sunday.
Monday morning we went back, and in the same day we're shipped back into the Union Station.
And there, nobody knew.
There was a troop-- a troop train.
And when we reported, and you just showed the people with the paper.
The commander of the troop train, he was a colonel.
He didn't know who the hell are these people there?
The nine of them.
What-- what-- what-- what we do with them?
But-- but it's official.
There is a stamp on it.
And so one day there are a lot of people there, army people, but who the hell were they?
Did you get uniforms?
Oh, yes.
Well, we've never changed the-- the insignia, Canadian insignia, but everything else.
There were one or two NCOs [INAUDIBLE] ordered the-- ordered the-- in the army.
There was one.
He was a-- he was a corporal in the armored-- with us in the armored corps.
And so he had to take just a Canadian insignia.
But we had that.
We carried right into the last moment, into-- into Italy, where then we passed into our destination.
No, well, then, the troop train went straight to-- straight to Halifax, and then we boarded
That ship there.
And it carried, my dear, 18,000 soldiers.
In-- in-- on the luxury-- luxury ship, there was only 4,600.
But they jammed 18,000 soldiers on it.
Oh, yes.
Well, we knew-- well, we knew that we were going overseas and there was going to be further things.
We just had the ground training, the-- the-- the initial training for parachute jumping because they haven't had that.
Coming a couple months later, then they had also parachute training in Orono area.
I don't know if you noticed it.
Yeah, yeah.
But we just had a ground training from-- from a tower.
You jumped.
But it was the same sensation as from-- from-- from-- from plane.
Oh, the was a real fast ship.
And we had escort, better ships and fighting planes, all to-- pulled out about 4 o'clock in the-- in the afternoon.
But the day-- the day was-- I don't know, I think it was-- it was Wednesday.
And it escorted us out about-- about 300 miles or something.
And then they just disappeared.
We were on our own.

But then it took a detour and zig-zagging, zig-zagging up to North Atlantic, North Atlantic, because the German submarines weren't fast enough to catch up unless they knew the exact route of the ship and they were waiting in ambush.
But they were dangerous.
But the survived the whole-- the whole war.
Mind you, they destroyed 4,000 ships in the North Atlantic from the American borders, the Canadian ports-- ports, and Canadian ports.
Yes.
